evals.name: dismissed-editor-ownership-proof
evals.prompt: EditorViewModel should deallocate after I dismiss an editor, but memory keeps growing. Design a Simulator memgraph investigation that proves the app-owned ownership path and verifies the eventual fix. I have two Simulators booted.
evals.expected_output: A lifetime-driven memgraph workflow that requires an explicit Simulator, captures one exact app process, preserves raw evidence, follows an app-owned type/path, and repeats the same flow after one ownership fix.
expectations: Defines dismissal plus legitimate asynchronous cleanup as the expected EditorViewModel release boundary.
Requires an explicit Simulator UDID because two devices are booted and rejects ambiguous process matches.
Captures or exports a `.memgraph` after the release boundary and preserves the graph plus raw leaks output and manifest.
Starts from the app-owned EditorViewModel or its allocation stack and uses bounded trace/reference/grouped evidence to find the first app-controlled strong edge.
Distinguishes an unreachable cycle from a still-reachable abandoned owner.
Verifies by repeating the identical flow and querying the same type/path rather than relying on RSS or graph size.

evals.name: zero-leaks-persistent-growth
evals.prompt: `leaks` reports zero, but every open/close cycle adds about 8 MB and it never falls. What should I do with baseline and post-flow memgraphs? Include how vmmap, heap, traceTree/referenceTree, and Malloc Stack Logging fit together.
evals.expected_output: A correction-focused workflow that treats zero leaks as compatible with reachable growth, uses comparable pre/post graphs to locate and classify heap growth, and chooses ownership or allocation evidence based on Malloc Stack Logging availability.
expectations: States that zero unreachable leaks does not rule out reachable but abandoned objects, caches, buffers, or heap regressions.
Uses matching baseline and post-flow graphs and `vmmap -summary` to locate the growing memory region.
Uses `heap --diffFrom=<baseline.memgraph> <post.memgraph>` to identify object types present in the post-flow heap but not the baseline.
Uses `heap --addresses=<pattern> <post.memgraph>` followed by `leaks --traceTree` or a grouped reference tree for ownership clues.

Uses a complete address-mode command such as `malloc_history post.memgraph -fullStacks <address>`, takes the address from `heap --addresses`, requires Malloc Stack Logging evidence, and keeps its setting identical across comparisons.
Preserves raw tool output and avoids treating text-parser columns as a stable API.

evals.name: weak-fix-claim-review
evals.prompt: I added `[weak self]` everywhere. The new graph file is smaller, RSS is down, and leaks reports 12 instead of 19 entries, so the retain-cycle bug is fixed. Review that conclusion and give me a proof standard.
evals.expected_output: A skeptical review that rejects aggregate metrics as ownership proof, requires a defined lifetime and app-owned path, evaluates weak/unowned semantics, and specifies same-flow repeated verification.
expectations: Rejects graph file size, one RSS reading, and aggregate leak count as sufficient proof.
Requires naming the object, expected release event, and original app-owned ownership path or isolated cycle.
Warns that blanket `[weak self]` can hide lifecycle reasoning and may change required work semantics.
Uses weak only when the owner may legitimately disappear and unowned only with a proven lifetime guarantee.
Requires matching builds, settings, flows, cleanup wait, and repeated captures.
Calls the fix supported only when the same type/path or attributable pre/post growth disappears.
